one_line_takeaway: A workshop paper releasing a reusable CIFAR-10/ResNet-9 benchmark of 2,200 precomputed model checkpoints and margins for standardizing machine-unlearning evaluation via the KLoM metric, plus a conceptual (unimplemented) proposal to extend KLoM to LLMs via teacher forcing.
key_finding: The paper's substantive finding is an ablation result, not a benchmark leaderboard: using boxplots of KLoM scores between pretrained and oracle models as a function of N (2 to 100) on CIFAR-10 forget sets 1-8, the authors conclude that N=100 compared models is sufficient for reliable KLoM estimation, even though the released dataset provides up to 200 oracle and 200 unlearned models per configuration -- i.e., doubling the ensemble size beyond 100 yields diminishing returns for metric stability. No comparative table of unlearning-method performance (e.g., noisy-SGD vs. other unlearning algorithms) with numeric KLoM scores is presented in the paper; the demo command and Python-API example illustrate usage rather than report evaluation results.
core_contribution: The paper introduces 'Easy Data Unlearning Bench' (EasyDUB), a benchmarking suite that packages precomputed model ensembles, oracle model outputs, and turnkey evaluation infrastructure to simplify empirical evaluation of machine-unlearning algorithms via the KLoM (KL divergence of Margins) metric from Georgiev et al. (2024). The suite ships a HuggingFace-hosted dataset of 200 pretrained ResNet-9 models on CIFAR-10, 10 forget sets (ranging from 10 to 1,000 samples, constructed via random, PCA-based, and CLIP-based selection), and 200 corresponding oracle models retrained on each retain set, plus precomputed classification margins for all models across validation, forget, and retain splits (2,200 model checkpoints total). Users can run a full unlearn-then-evaluate pipeline with a single command (`uv run python demo.py --method noisy_descent --forget-set 1 --n-models 100`) or compute KLoM directly from precomputed margins without a GPU. The authors also propose an extension of KLoM to autoregressive language models ('Teacher-forcing KLoM'), defined by averaging per-token margin-histogram KL divergence between oracle and unlearned model ensembles under teacher forcing, as a conceptual future-work direction rather than an implemented/evaluated component of the released benchmark.
threat_model: Not applicable. This is a benchmarking/evaluation-infrastructure paper for machine unlearning quality (statistical closeness of an unlearned model's output distribution to a retrained-from-scratch oracle); it defines no adversary, attacker capability, or security threat model. The paper does reference membership-inference attacks (Carlini et al., 2022) and U-LiRA (Hayes et al., 2024) as a prior *evaluation methodology* it argues is inferior/gameable, but this is a discussion of measurement validity, not a security threat the paper's own contribution defends against or exploits.
limitations: The paper is explicit that it is a workshop-length benchmark/infrastructure release (ICML 2025 Workshop on Machine Unlearning for Generative AI) rather than a full evaluation study: it provides only one dataset/architecture pair (CIFAR-10 with ResNet-9), acknowledges that broader dataset and model-size coverage is left to future community contributions, and states that its 'Teacher-forcing KLoM' extension to language models is a proposed future direction described conceptually in Section 2.3 -- it is not implemented, released, or empirically evaluated in this paper. The authors also note planned-but-not-yet-included extensions: incorporating the Gaussian Unlearning Score (GUS) metric and a public leaderboard akin to RobustBench. The scope section itself states the benchmark 'focuses on classification models evaluated via predictive margins under the logistic loss' and that generative-model relevance is only 'conceptually aligned,' not demonstrated.

datasets_used: 10 forget sets ranging from 10 to 1,000 samples, constructed via random, PCA-based, and CLIP-based selection (reused from Georgiev et al., 2024, 'Attribute-to-Delete')
quantitative_results: 200 pretrained (full-data) ResNet-9 models on CIFAR-10 provided by the dataset
200 oracle models (retrained on retain set) per forget set provided by the dataset
2,200 total model checkpoints and their precomputed margins hosted on HuggingFace

KLoM hyperparameters: N=100 oracle and 100 unlearned models used by default (up to 200 available); 20 histogram bins; margin clipping range [-100, 100]; smoothing constant epsilon=1e-5, capping max KLoM at approximately 12

Appendix A.1 ablation: KLoM vs. number of compared models N swept from 2 to 100 on CIFAR-10 forget sets 1-8, reporting the 95th-percentile KLoM at each N across validation, forget, and retain sets; conclusion is that N=100 is sufficient
A typical new unlearning-method integration requires fewer than 50 lines of code
baselines_compared: U-LiRA (Hayes et al., 2024) -- discussed and critiqued as a prior evaluation metric (susceptible to constant-margin gaming, misaligned with the formal (epsilon,delta)-unlearning definition, and obscures compound forget-set effects), not empirically benchmarked against KLoM with numbers in this paper
Retraining without the forget set (the oracle) is described as the standardized 'strong baseline' the benchmark evaluates unlearning methods against
The quick-start example runs 'noisy_descent' as an example unlearning method but reports no numeric KLoM result for it in the paper text
